skill.name: event-store-design
skill.description: Design event store architectures for event-sourced systems, including storage choices, schemas, subscriptions, snapshots, and replay safety.
skill.summary: Plan reliable event stores with practical patterns for storage, ordering, concurrency, and projections.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported weak cryptography, reconnaissance, external command, and network patterns. Review found Markdown code fences, domain terminology, SQL and Python examples, database fetch calls, and documentation links rather than malicious behavior. Risk is low because copyable examples can connect to EventStoreDB or AWS services only when a user chooses to run them.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts match ordinary words and headings such as the skill name, description, and design playbook title. No evidence found of DES, MD5, SHA1, or other weak cryptographic algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain event store documentation text, headings, and table labels. They do not contain cryptographic APIs, hashing calls, encryption routines, or algorithm configuration.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Reported as Backticks
low_findings.description: The external command alerts correspond to Markdown inline code and fenced code blocks around diagrams, SQL, and Python examples. No evidence found of shell execution, Ruby backticks, command interpolation, or assistant instructions to run commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each location is within a Markdown documentation block. The snippets define schemas and example classes, but the skill does not execute them or invoke a shell.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms Are Domain Fields
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts match database field names, indexes, stream identifiers, and checkpoint terms used in event store examples. No evidence found of host enumeration, system probing, or environment discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are SQL columns, indexes, and event stream parameters. They do not call operating system tools or request machine, network, user, or process inventory.

low_findings.title: Benign Network-Capable Example Code
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples that can connect to a local EventStoreDB instance and an AWS DynamoDB table when copied into an application. The reviewed content does not send secrets to external endpoints or perform hidden network activity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch alerts at lines 209 and 229 are async database method calls, while lines 447-449 are documentation URLs. Lines 308 and 379-380 are legitimate database client examples that require user adoption and configuration.

content.value_statement: Event-sourced systems need reliable append-only storage, ordering, and replay behavior. This skill helps Claude, Codex, or Claude Code compare options and draft practical event store designs.
actual_capabilities: Compare EventStoreDB, PostgreSQL, Kafka, DynamoDB, and Marten for event store use.
Draft PostgreSQL schemas for streams, event metadata, snapshots, and subscription checkpoints.
Provide Python examples for append, stream read, global read, and subscription patterns.
Explain optimistic concurrency, idempotency, ordering, snapshots, and projection rebuilds.
Outline guardrails for schema versioning, retention, archival, and replay safety.
limitations: Does not run migrations, provision databases, or validate live infrastructure.
Example code needs adaptation, testing, and security review before production use.
Focuses on event store design, not full domain modeling or user interface workflow design.
Does not replace vendor documentation for managed service limits or pricing.

use_cases.title: Select an event store platform
use_cases.description: Compare storage technologies against throughput, query, operations, and ecosystem needs.

use_cases.title: Design a custom event table
use_cases.description: Create a schema plan for streams, versions, metadata, snapshots, and subscriptions.

use_cases.title: Plan replay and projection safety
use_cases.description: Define checkpointing, idempotent handlers, rebuild steps, and operational guardrails.

prompt_templates.title: Compare storage options
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner evaluation of event store choices.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose an event store for a new event-sourced service. Compare EventStoreDB, PostgreSQL, Kafka, and DynamoDB for my needs.

prompt_templates.title: Design a PostgreSQL event store
prompt_templates.scenario: Schema planning for a relational implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a PostgreSQL event store for my aggregate streams. Include event fields, metadata, indexes, snapshots, and subscription checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Review an event store design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my event store design for concurrency, ordering, idempotency, replay behavior, and projection safety. Identify risks and missing decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Plan production operations
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for scale and recovery.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an operational plan for an event store with high write volume, long-lived aggregates, projection rebuilds, retention, and archival requirements.

output_examples.input: We need an event store for order aggregates in PostgreSQL.
output_examples.output: A recommended design with stream identifiers, version checks, global positions, metadata fields, indexes, snapshots, and checkpoint storage.

output_examples.input: We are comparing EventStoreDB and DynamoDB for serverless workloads.
output_examples.output: A tradeoff summary covering query patterns, ordering guarantees, operational burden, ecosystem fit, and known limitations.

output_examples.input: Our projections must support full replay after a schema change.
output_examples.output: A replay plan with idempotent handlers, checkpoint reset steps, backpressure controls, monitoring points, and schema version guidance.

best_practices: Start with clear stream boundaries, expected concurrency rules, and replay requirements.
Keep event payloads small and include correlation, causation, and schema version metadata.
Test projection rebuilds and subscription checkpoint recovery before production launch.
anti_patterns: Using an event store as a mutable reporting database.
Skipping optimistic concurrency checks on aggregate stream writes.
Choosing a storage platform before defining query and replay requirements.

faq.question: Can this skill generate production-ready code?
faq.answer: It provides starting examples and design guidance. Review, test, and adapt all code for your runtime, security, and operational requirements.

faq.question: Does it support EventStoreDB?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes EventStoreDB guidance and example usage for appending, reading, subscriptions, and category projections.

faq.question: Can it help with PostgreSQL event stores?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers relational schemas, stream version uniqueness, global ordering, snapshots, and subscription checkpoints.

faq.question: Is this only for CQRS systems?
faq.answer: No. It is useful for event-sourced systems with or without a formal CQRS architecture.

faq.question: Can it decide between Kafka and an event store?
faq.answer: Yes. It can compare Kafka with event store technologies based on stream queries, ordering, throughput, and operational needs.

faq.question: Does it handle event schema evolution?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes versioned events, upcaster policies, retention planning, and replay-safe projection rebuilds.
